Sure.
I'll kick off several hooks here.
Do you guys want to worry about Mejuri or just jump straight to?
I have context.
It's just mostly like if you want to give just a little bit of context on the why now, especially in your role and kind of the purpose in the business right now to drive growth.
I'll focus more on that.
So I support all the product engineering and most of the marketing departments here in Mejuri.
So when you think about the growth marketing, Google search, social media, influencer, the retention, they all fall under my scope.
So that's one part.
The part where why now?
I mean, it's.
Actually, right, like I'm spending a lot of time thinking about like where to go from here, like how to leverage like new technologies and like in different ways, right?
So one is like consumer shopping behavior is changing.
How do I tackle that on Google?
It's like meta, Google, they're always doing like all this new enhancements, products and features.
So staying on top of that.
So Tim does a really good job there.
But I'm constantly looking at what else can we do, right?

Like, for example, can I enhance my and what's my product catch up to get more out of Google, looking at personalization journeys and all that good stuff, personalization on emails, text forward emails, a bunch of other stuff there.
Yeah.
So that's just one, right?
Like how do you get more upside?
The other part is just how do we do more with the people on the team?
I think this is where I've personally not spent enough time looking at like automations that we can do here or are they more valued activities?
If we can increase the velocity, including some of the tech.
So again, because I manage engineering as well, I'm able to kind of move in and out of different departments and just not really quickly.
And at times when I just tried to do hands-on and build a point of view, so I just bumped into it.
I think it was a LinkedIn post or something, and I saw growth as I was like, you know, I've been thinking along these lines as well.
So let's see what are you guys doing, and if there's a plug-and-play or some sort of like partnership opportunity over here, like worst case, like walk away with friends and maybe a couple of ideas.
I love it.
I love this space as well.
Maybe I can give you just a high-level one, and because you're also on the engineering side, I can also show you more of that underneath the hood than I normally would if you're interested in that.
But I can give you like the high-level story, which is background on how we got started.
what it is.
I don't
I've been in large enterprises and brands and almost every time we were spending way too much money in ads and paid channels and I realized we need to grow organically.

In order to grow organically, a lot of marketing content was just empty and so I always thought about how can brands be more like publishers and more like creators, if you will, to build trust with their audiences.
And so I did that throughout my whole career and more recently in my previous role as CMO, I've been in the AI space for a little while.
was at Skill AI and then DeepGram in the voice space, I started to kind of take a lot of the best practices.
So think of it as like, how do I identify the right opportunities?
Which pages do we improve?
And, you know, and essentially distilling them into AI workflows, right?
And after going through that process, over and over again, it created a lot of value for the company with 4x revenue and 24x traffic.
And that led to me just doing a lot of workshops.

Teaching people how to build workflows and think through their growth strategies, but then quickly realized as we were going through is that people didn't want to have this full-time job of building workflows, maintaining these workflows, and doing all of this, right?
And it was kind of that cold start problem where you go into NAN or whatever tool, there's many good ones out there, and you're just like, okay, how do I bake in my expertise into this workflow?
But then you spend most of your time actually building the workflows, and even if you're doing it in code, just getting to the basic of things.
And then by the time you're done with the basics of things, you're actually not baking in a lot of the expertise that you want to replicate, right?
And so I can show you a little bit of kind of how we approached this here in a second, but the TLDR is we saw that coding agents were just getting better and better.
So we built our own coding agent that builds workflows in code, and then a runtime layer on top of that.
And then now...

We a learn engine that we're building on top of all of that, but ultimately the reason we're doing everything as a service is because things are shifting just too quickly that if we try to launch a platform, a generalized platform for building workflows or whatnot, just, we would get the wrong signals of what to build, right?
So when we deliver as a service and have forward deploy AI engineers and infrastructure teams, right?
Like we're building for us and we're ultimately signing up to deliver the whole thing.
And then over time, we'll expose more and more to our clients.
And that makes sense.
Like that's what I'm hearing from the VCs as well.
Like that's the service cluster model, right?
Like again, like it's things evolving so fast.
Exactly.
And so just to give you like a high level, you're more technical.
So you'll understand a little bit, but a lot of these are, these are all the different workflows and we're constantly generating, but this is all like auto-documented.
Coding agent.
And what it does is it's going to go through a bunch of different steps.
And this is just a flowchart that's auto-generated based on the logic behind it.
But what it does is we have over 70 different APIs and we're constantly adding them.
And so it kind of removes a lot of the, if you will, burden on teams to try to figure out what are the latest APIs for creating content or for doing research.
And so, and then all the best practices around prompting, inputs.
And so everything we're doing is building workflows, but on behalf of customers, but running them, but all with the right context and the right strategy.
Like, so as opposed to just in a blanket say, hey, I need a workflow that does this.
And it's like, there's no purpose behind it.
It's usually around either content, PDP enrichment.
It's around like growth initiatives, right?
And so just to give an example here, all of these are.
We have this kind of four-file system, if you will, that has all the activities, the prompts, the types, the workflows.
And then all of these workflows are running in our runtime layer.
And so if you look at any of these, let me actually find one, deep research, or one just to show you.
So this one, for example, is for one of our customers called School AI, and it has all the context on the customer already.
So in your case, it can have all the context around your company, around even the product category.
You can have context on anything that we define, right?
And this one is researching, like, this topic over here, for instance, right?
And so this workflow specifically is going to go and generate a bunch of research questions.
And then what we're doing is thinking multiple APIs, like Perplexities API.

And instead of just getting Perplexities answer, we're actually going straight to the citations, and then scraping through the citations and filtering which ones are actually relevant to you based on the context we've had it, right?
And then after doing all of that, we're vectorizing, putting in a vector DB, and using it for retrieval downstream as we're generating all that content, right?

So then what it ends up looking like is think of this as if you had unlimited resources from someone on your team who's actually an expert and is going through this process, what would be the process you would design, right?

If everyone had a limited time, right, you would go and break it down into first principles and saying like, hey, if we go here and let's just see, like, how do I improve like a PDP or something, right?
Like, how would I enrich it?
It's like, well, I would add more taxonomy around it.
I would improve the description.
I would have more on the details.
I would, you know, like be more descriptive on the materials or whatever, right?
Like you probably have data on like what improves the quality and the conversion rates of it.
Of these pages, for instance, right?
And then you say, okay, how would I go about doing that?
I would do, you know, I would go and I would research.
I would, you know, for each material, I would go and do research times a thousand, you know, and I would go find, like, Reddit posts or forums where people are mentioning this.
I would think about, like, the different uses of this style or the difference, whatever, right?
Like, I'm not the expert in your space, but then how would you run that times a thousand consistently, you know, and then put the right processes in place?
And that's kind of what we do, for instance, like, we're talking to the real real, right?

Like, there's a lot of, like, the needs are pretty similar, both editorial, but then within e-commerce, there's also the same needs, which is ultimately, how do you go, fetch the right information, process it the right way, and then make sure that it's on brand, the right
Format and can be used, right?
So hopefully that gives you a little bit of high level, but we'll be curious, like, thoughts and also specific use cases you're thinking through.
Yeah, this makes a lot of sense, like, and from the use case perspective, I'm about to ask you, like, where are you seeing most success, right?
So if I think about this PDP example, right, like, I can see that it can help my product managers, but maybe once in a quarter, right?
Like, it will help them see, hey, here are the two or three opportunities, they will have some directional ideas around A-B tests that they want to learn.

But for a company like us, where I've been doing, like, a bunch of A-B tests on our PDPs for almost, like, two years now, there's not a lot of, like, step change opportunities, like, if that makes sense.
So if I have limited resources, I'm actually thinking less about PDP, but thinking more about personalization on PLP.
Just using that example, and on PDP, I'm thinking more innovation where I don't see others.
Taking that approach, I'm rethinking PDP completely, right?
Got it, okay.

Where I see opportunities are more internal as well, like how can I run more customer segmentation analysis, like are there customer simulation that I can do is like how will customers behave, can I do competitor research, right, like what sort of competitor ads do I see on Meta, like what is, Pandora and Goryana doing, like that level of deep research is where I'll be interested in, like if I were to use one specific use case where I see it takes us a lot of time and it's probably needed like every two, three months and it's a big exercise, right?
Yeah, yeah, exactly.
I think what's really cool with this is like we've actually done exactly this.
Let me just show you on the analyzing.
So there's a company we work with, they're not in e-commerce.
They're, they're,
Coding agent called Bolt.new.
I don't know if you're familiar, but one of the challenges their data science teams had were around, like, essentially enriching the session data they had.
And so if you look at, like, a lot of the session data, it just looks kind of like this, you know?
And then the output, we were able to generate an output that made a lot more sense, essentially, right?
Like, and enriched.
And so what this does, summary, right?

So, for instance, initially planned a luxury jive-matching mobile app for Monaco using a Tinder-style interface, targeted high-end sectors, overcame multiple errors, then after a 22-day break, shifted focus to creating a futuristic AI tech website using Astro and Tailwind with the specific design elements and color pellets.
Employed the site, made various UI adjustment, and converted to pay subscriptions within 15 days of reactivation.

What we're doing here is we took every single session they had for, you know, like, and you can see here, like, a bunch of them for people that converted, and then we processed that and actually, like, described it better what happened.
And then from there, we were able to figure out, like, what are the key insights much better than just, like, raw, like, usage data, essentially.
This is amazing, right?
Like, this is one of the areas, like, just to give you a sense, like, where I'm pushing my team to look at the event stream and the clickstream, right?
Like, this is, like, becoming so much easier with the LAMs now, right?

Like, your point what you're doing here, like, if I can look at all the clickstream and build personas based on all the products they clicked and looked at, whether it's color or whether it's the price point or the material that they're looking at, like, now you have personas based on the actual use of behavior on the website that I try to suss out with mixpanel as well as, like, hot job recording sessions.
Like, you can imagine how much time it takes that no one will.
Does it?
Right?
Exactly.
And you know what was crazy off the record is that when we did this, they had an entire data science team.
And I think what we did in one day was probably more valuable than months of their analysis.
They were just building dashboards, right?
It's like what the estimate would with more dashboards.
And you can see this is the workflow that drove what I just showed you, the input validation, summary activity, and then categories provided, return summary, blah, blah, blah.

It's doing that within the context of you, your company, or if you want to do by category, or if you want to do by however you're segmenting your business, right?
You can give it that extra set of context and analyze it.
But then you need to run a time, 10,000, 100,000, whatever, and then summarize the summary or whatever you want to do.
But for us, what ended up happening...
happening here, just to kind of show you what we built.

And then we want to actually build the exact app itself.

And I think this is relevant because like essentially like finding the right insights, but then applying it to whatever it is programmatically is super, super valuable.
And it's like what's really relevant for us, like what we're finding is what people need, even though it's very different, but at the very core, it's very similar.
Oh, meaning.

And it's kind of like the same.
The scaffolding doesn't really change, right?
Yeah.
The first principles perspective is the same set of activities.
Yeah.
I've broken it out.
Let me ask, give you this year's case.
I just want to hear how you think about this.
So one of the areas that I've been thinking about is enriching my product catalog, right?
Thinking about the taxonomy and the search queries that is closer to the natural language, right?
Like when you're not shopping, you're not shopping the way a catalog is written out, like with all the metadata and all the attributes.
The idea of searching in a very different way, looking for a shirt at this price range or something.
want to to

What it is anything, any AI features you want to build, right, they're, what we're finding is they're kind of being distilled into the same thing, which is ultimately, like, inputs, different APIs, LLM calls, you know, some level of retrieval, mix and replay that in some ways, right, and what, how you define these features is no longer in the code, like, some of our workflows, code is, like, 12 lines, you know, it's like, it's not, it's not a lot, right, but what you're defining is, like, ultimately, like, kind of the prompts and how you mix all of this and the instructions and what context you feed it and all of that, right, and what you don't want to do is go through the process we've gone through of, like, building a runtime layer, like, stitching it all together, like, building the workflows, you want to know, you want to take the logic and how you want to approach, given your business knowledge and the context you have, and you want to quickly iterate, like, so, for instance, like, we built a fact checker workflow, and the fact checker checks against either a database or a retrieval, so, for
Chunks an output into facts and then goes into each one of those facts and tries to find a backup to make sure it's factual or not, right?
And gives you a confidence score.
So that's an example of one that if we're building that as a feature, it could take months.
It took us a day to build, right?
And so the way to think about it is building AI features, you know, and all our workflows are endpoint APIs.
And once you've validated that, then you can actually like productize those or run it at an even bigger scale if needed.
But like our runtime layer, we're running millions of runs a month already.
And our co-founder, CTO, he built, was head of product and engineering at IFT.
I don't know if you remember if this, then that.
So like, you know, 50 million users, like so pretty, pretty large.
And then what I would say is like when I was at Scale AI, we did a lot of project e-commerce projects.
So for instance, like Walmart, Meta paid us $30 million to go launch 10,000 storefronts.
So when...
They were trying to launch Instagram shopping, you know, and most of that was exactly what you were saying.
Like, it wasn't just like, hey, this is a shirt.
It was like, what's the season?
Like, what's the, you know, so you're kind of like developing and every item is different, right?
Like a shirt versus like a phone case versus whatever, like they're all like different taxonomy and you need to enrich that to the max.
We need to enrich that within the context of the user intent behind it, right?
And what it's going to be, you know, and that engine is like really hard, but you also need humans in the loop, which is the part that we do as well, you know?

Yeah, and that's kind of like in this particular example, what I was thinking about when I was chatting with that vendor was like, what is this specific domain knowledge that they're bringing to the table, right?
Like, what do they know that they don't know right now?
I think that that's what you guys would need as well, right?
Like, you can go scrape all those sources, but someone needs to say, hey, what's the, what's the vibe for this sort of style that we are producing?
Yeah, like, but I think the, you're right.
And I would would say.

Because the way we started was around editorial and content, the bar for editorial is so high to be on brand and the knowledge that like to apply it to programmatic things, which is like catalog enrichment or like personalization or like batching the right data is like, like I said, it's like the components under the hood are fairly similar.
So to produce, yeah, anyways, yeah.
Right.
I know we only have five minutes now.
do have a hot shot at 1.30.
What would be the best way for us to follow up?
My head of growth and my head of engineering as well for the call.
And if you guys can give me like a couple of any guidance on like where do you guys see success for e-commerce brands, then I can ask them to focus on those specific brands come up with more.
That's perfect.
Yeah.
Let's let's do that.
So I'll give you just a quick TLDR on kind of how we do engagements now just because.
Especially after our Series A, like we got a bit of overwhelming demand and we try to be really selective with brands that we work with.
And so what we do now is a strategy sprint.
It's eight weeks and no long-term commitment.
And it's like a mutual opt-out, meaning like we can opt out, you can opt out at the end of it.
And the reason we do that is because we want to define a lot of these use cases while delivering like real deliverables in that process.
And so it just makes it a lot easier than trying to spend the next three months perfectly defining everything, you know.
And so it's very similar to how like MetaLab and others would work, you know.
And then from there we go into like growth execution mode, but it's all defined within that sprint.
And we try to go as hard as possible.
And so for you, what I would recommend is we do a one-hour deep dive just to kind of go deeper into some of those use cases.
And then we'll do a lot more research on your brand, you know.
And on our end, on what we see externally.
And then after that, like, that will give us a better sense of, like, if we do decide mutually that we want to go into a strategy sprint, that's, you know, no risk pretty much.
And we can schedule you.
And the only other thing I'll say really quickly is just that we're usually about six weeks out just due to demand.
And so just something to keep in mind of, like, what we try to do is remove the burden so we don't have a six-month sales cycle.
But then because of that ends up having a lot of, like, demand, but we limit the number of engagements we take on.
Yeah, timing is not a problem for me.
I think we have a board meeting next week.
So the earliest we can reconnect, like, especially for an hour, would be, I'll say, towards the end of next week anyway.
I can give a time to my team, and then we can follow up ASync over the email.
Perfect.
I just want to make sure, like, we, to your point, right, like, we try to make the best out of that an hour.
We can come up with, like, use cases that's on top of
And then see if there's this eight-week partnership opportunity here.
Yeah, I personally think it's little helpful for the group, so let's show this now.
And I can share one last insight, which was when I was at Scaleway AI, one of our advisors, board members, was Jeff Wollner.
I don't know if you know him.
He was the CEO of Amazon Consumer for 25 years.
And he mentioned every year we'd spend at least a billion in catalog.
And it was the best billion I spent, and I wish we were spending two or three every year in catalog.
Amazon catalog used to be a beast.
Like, I was at Amazon Fresh, and we were not able to move fast because of the Amazon.com catalog.
So Amazon.com catalog was so rigid, and Amazon Fresh, you can imagine, like, for grocery, had, like, very unique requirements.
So we used to go to the catalog team, and, like, nope, like, any changes to the catalog?
Look, it's not happening.
No changes to the catalog will be approved without, like, basically, jet-level alignment.
So, you know, you've been on that side already.
I'm actually going through, like, catalog upgrade migration conversation internally, and I'm coaching my team guys, like, this is not a plug-and-play.
Like, we really need to think really hard about, like, creating a flexible.
Yeah.
That's perfect.
And so on the next call, would love to bring Dave.
He was head of SEO at TripAdvisor for several years, and before that, he was at Fanatics for 13 years and built it from a mom-and-pop shop to $600 million a year.
And so I'd love to do more on the SEO and technical SEO side, but I think it's really relevant, obviously, to everything you would care about as well.
And so I can bring him along in the next call so that we can bring some additional insights as well.
Awesome.
That sounds good.
Perfect.
Thank you so We'll follow up via email.
Thank you so much for the time and excited.
Headed to chat some more next week.
Thanks so much, guys.
Take care.
Thank you.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Introduce GrowthX's AI-powered growth services to Mejuri and explore potential collaboration opportunities.](https://fathom.video/share/yewPm9ETnx3MMaQ-goTHP6FBEhzg_7Pa?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [GrowthX offers AI-driven workflows for content creation, research, and e-commerce optimization, with a focus on delivering as a service rather than a platform](https://fathom.video/share/yewPm9ETnx3MMaQ-goTHP6FBEhzg_7Pa?tab=summary&timestamp=163.0)
  - [Rohit (Mejuri) is interested in leveraging AI for customer segmentation, competitor research, and catalog enrichment to improve natural language search](https://fathom.video/share/yewPm9ETnx3MMaQ-goTHP6FBEhzg_7Pa?tab=summary&timestamp=610.0)
  - [Both parties agreed to schedule a deeper 1-hour follow-up call to discuss specific use cases and potentially initiate an 8-week strategy sprint](https://fathom.video/share/yewPm9ETnx3MMaQ-goTHP6FBEhzg_7Pa?tab=summary&timestamp=1295.0)

## Topics

### GrowthX's AI-Powered Service Overview

  - [Built a coding agent that creates workflows in code with a runtime layer on top](https://fathom.video/share/yewPm9ETnx3MMaQ-goTHP6FBEhzg_7Pa?tab=summary&timestamp=360.0)
  - [Utilizes 70+ APIs for content creation, research, and data processing](https://fathom.video/share/yewPm9ETnx3MMaQ-goTHP6FBEhzg_7Pa?tab=summary&timestamp=369.0)
  - [Focuses on delivering results as a service, with forward-deployed AI engineers and infrastructure teams](https://fathom.video/share/yewPm9ETnx3MMaQ-goTHP6FBEhzg_7Pa?tab=summary&timestamp=317.0)
  - [Adapts quickly to evolving AI landscape, avoiding limitations of a generalized platform](https://fathom.video/share/yewPm9ETnx3MMaQ-goTHP6FBEhzg_7Pa?tab=summary&timestamp=280.0)

### Mejuri's Growth Priorities

  - [Rohit oversees product engineering and most marketing departments](https://fathom.video/share/yewPm9ETnx3MMaQ-goTHP6FBEhzg_7Pa?tab=summary&timestamp=37.0)
  - Key focus areas:
      - [Adapting to changing consumer shopping behavior](https://fathom.video/share/yewPm9ETnx3MMaQ-goTHP6FBEhzg_7Pa?tab=summary&timestamp=68.0)
      - [Leveraging new technologies and features from platforms like Google and Meta](https://fathom.video/share/yewPm9ETnx3MMaQ-goTHP6FBEhzg_7Pa?tab=summary&timestamp=72.0)
      - [Enhancing product catalog and personalization journeys](https://fathom.video/share/yewPm9ETnx3MMaQ-goTHP6FBEhzg_7Pa?tab=summary&timestamp=85.0)
      - [Improving team efficiency through automation and value-added activities](https://fathom.video/share/yewPm9ETnx3MMaQ-goTHP6FBEhzg_7Pa?tab=summary&timestamp=102.0)

### Use Case Exploration

  - [Customer segmentation analysis and simulation](https://fathom.video/share/yewPm9ETnx3MMaQ-goTHP6FBEhzg_7Pa?tab=summary&timestamp=667.0)
  - [Competitor research, especially ad strategies on platforms like Meta](https://fathom.video/share/yewPm9ETnx3MMaQ-goTHP6FBEhzg_7Pa?tab=summary&timestamp=667.0)
  - [Catalog enrichment to improve natural language search capabilities](https://fathom.video/share/yewPm9ETnx3MMaQ-goTHP6FBEhzg_7Pa?tab=summary&timestamp=993.0)
  - [Clickstream analysis for building user personas based on actual website behavior](https://fathom.video/share/yewPm9ETnx3MMaQ-goTHP6FBEhzg_7Pa?tab=summary&timestamp=803.0)

### GrowthX's Relevant Experience

  - [Worked with Bolt.new to enrich session data, providing valuable insights beyond raw usage data](https://fathom.video/share/yewPm9ETnx3MMaQ-goTHP6FBEhzg_7Pa?tab=summary&timestamp=741.0)
  - [Experience with large-scale e-commerce projects (e.g., Walmart, Meta's Instagram shopping launch)](https://fathom.video/share/yewPm9ETnx3MMaQ-goTHP6FBEhzg_7Pa?tab=summary&timestamp=1191.0)
  - [Strong background in editorial content, which translates well to e-commerce catalog enrichment](https://fathom.video/share/yewPm9ETnx3MMaQ-goTHP6FBEhzg_7Pa?tab=summary&timestamp=582.0)

### Engagement Process

  - [8-week strategy sprint with no long-term commitment (mutual opt-out option)](https://fathom.video/share/yewPm9ETnx3MMaQ-goTHP6FBEhzg_7Pa?tab=summary&timestamp=1328.0)
  - [Defines use cases while delivering real results during the process](https://fathom.video/share/yewPm9ETnx3MMaQ-goTHP6FBEhzg_7Pa?tab=summary&timestamp=1350.0)
  - [Typically 6 weeks out due to high demand](https://fathom.video/share/yewPm9ETnx3MMaQ-goTHP6FBEhzg_7Pa?tab=summary&timestamp=1396.0)
  - [Selective about partnering with brands](https://fathom.video/share/yewPm9ETnx3MMaQ-goTHP6FBEhzg_7Pa?tab=summary&timestamp=1328.0)

## Next Steps

  - [Schedule a 1-hour deep dive call for the following week](https://fathom.video/share/yewPm9ETnx3MMaQ-goTHP6FBEhzg_7Pa?tab=summary&timestamp=1369.0)
  - [GrowthX to bring Dave (former TripAdvisor SEO head) to provide additional e-commerce and SEO insights](https://fathom.video/share/yewPm9ETnx3MMaQ-goTHP6FBEhzg_7Pa?tab=summary&timestamp=1528.0)
  - [GrowthX team to research Mejuri's brand and prepare specific e-commerce use cases for discussion](https://fathom.video/share/yewPm9ETnx3MMaQ-goTHP6FBEhzg_7Pa?tab=summary&timestamp=1375.0)
  - [Explore potential for an 8-week strategy sprint partnership](https://fathom.video/share/yewPm9ETnx3MMaQ-goTHP6FBEhzg_7Pa?tab=summary&timestamp=1447.0)

action_items.description: Schedule 1hr follow-up deep dive w/ GrowthX team for end of next week (post-board meeting). Prep use cases to discuss.
Bring Dave (ex-TripAdvisor SEO head) to next call w/ Mejuri. Prep for deeper SEO/tech SEO discussion.
